chapters.title: What-if questions that sap your energy
chapters.text:
Anxiety is apprehension, disquiet, and uneasiness. It lives a life of its own, taking over your consciousness and making you suspicious and terrified at the same time. Anxiety is also a bunch of the most irrational feelings. For example, you are taking a walk and suddenly feel that you will fall. You are healthy; your head is not dizzy. Nothing threatens you. This on-the-spot emotional experience might make you gulp, and your legs turn to lead, with your heart pumping. You will either accelerate the pace or stand nailed to the spot. At this point, your mind is busy producing worst-case scenarios, from creepy to horrifying. You think something dreadful is about to happen. But it never does.

Fear deals with threats; anxiety makes them up.

Recent statistics from the National Institute of Mental Health highlight a concerning trend: anxiety is on the rise, affecting around 15% of the population. Its disorders comprise panic attacks, phobias, and social and separation anxiety. Attacks may vary in severity; for instance, some people feel lightheaded. While you can alleviate the discomfort with measured breathing and sipping water, other episodes can cause severe panic. A person might be unable to breathe with a temporary loss of eyesight. Women are more prone to anxiety disorders than men. For them, alcohol and drug abuse outstrip anxiety disorders.

The US government spends $300 billion each year on healthcare bills and lost productivity. Meanwhile, the usage of sedative drugs keeps increasing; between 1997 and 2004, tranquilizers like Xanax and Valium became twice as popular. Their purchase went from $900 million to $2.1 billion. This rate is alarming.

The presence of anxiety is unavoidable, but the prison of anxiety is optional. ~ Max Lucado

What causes anxiety?

Rapidly changing communication technologies and the advent of the Internet have brought about increasing warnings about terrorist attacks, nuclear war, and global warming, among others. With gadgets like smartphones and tablets, we scroll through these messages every ten minutes. Our parents had to wait until late in the evening to hear the news; now, all information is at our fingertips. As we try to process one crisis, we hear about another.

Did you know? If friends and family members tell you that you have been gloomy and critical for a long time, you might be dealing with anxiety.

chapters.title: You can’t control everything
chapters.text:
Apostle Paul had a reason to be anxious. Imagine an older man stuck in a Roman prison; he was 60 and had only spent 30 years as a Christian. Imprisoned for his miraculous deeds, he received 39 lashes on five different occasions, and on three of them, executioners beat him with rods. His body, with scars winding across his skin, told the story of a man who faced extreme adversity: betrayal by those he trusted, the harshness of prison, near-death experiences, and the brutal forces of nature. Yet the challenges didn't end — his trial loomed.

Meanwhile, the churches he established were in disarray. Internal disputes were rampant, compounded by the emergence of self-serving false prophets, fueling division with their teachings that lacked humility and generosity. Life wasn't easy for the Apostle. He had a gloomy future, as he could not look past the jail’s window. Yet, if you read his words, he sounded like he had arrived at a resort.

Apostle Paul’s letter to the Philippians contained no word of complaint. He never got angry at God; on the contrary, the essence of his message lies in being grateful and choosing to rejoice in the Lord. It was his solution to the problem of anxiety: don’t try to fight it directly — at first, rejoice!

But how can you obey the commandment always to rejoice? Can we maintain an infinite spirit of gladness? No! Apostle Paul's message wasn't about feeling that increases your heartbeat or “butterflies” in your stomach. He meant a deep-seated belief and trust in God's control, no matter what challenges you face.

Apostle Paul's unwavering spirit reflected the strength of his beliefs. How strong are yours? Look into your soul. You will see an array of ideas supporting and guiding you through life. These beliefs form the backbone of your understanding of fundamental existential questions, such as the purpose and value of life and whether there is more beyond your earthly existence.

Trust in the Lord helps ease your burdens and makes room for hope.

Apostle Paul, in his writings, consistently emphasized the importance of these beliefs. He suggested that to alter our reactions to life's challenges, we must first reevaluate what we think about them.

chapters.title: Choose grace over guilt
chapters.text:
Apostle Paul nurtured his grace, relying on God’s mercy. He had all the reasons in the world to feel guilt. He was once a figure of terror in the early Christian community, known for his relentless pursuit of believers, invading homes, and showing no mercy in imprisoning both men and women for their faith.

He spilled the blood of many innocent Christians and then came the landmark Damascus event. Jesus appeared before him. Once Paul set his eyes upon Jesus, he became blind. His previous deeds seemed to have no value or meaning any longer. There was no reason to boast about killing Christians anymore. He could not see any option other than to commit his life to Jesus. Therefore, he dedicated his life to talking about Jesus and being humble about his persona.

Paul acknowledged his crimes but chose not to dwell in guilt. Instead, he turned to Jesus for solace and redemption. He believed in setting aside the past to concentrate on the future, a journey he likened to a race toward heaven.

Many people hide their unresolved regrets behind the mask of ferocity.

Are you a guilt-laden person? What would the Apostle’s advice be to you today? “You can keep trying to save yourself, but you needn’t do it alone. Leave yourself open for God and let His grace enter your tormented soul.”

Instead of being caught in endless introspection, we should look ahead and focus our energy on things we can still influence, not on the irreversible past. It's crucial to remember that despite our imperfections and wrongdoings, we can always rely on the goodness and forgiveness of God, who sacrificed for humanity's sins. God’s grace is available today. It is capable of embracing and absolving our sins.

Paul's story is an invitation to start anew, to embrace the future with hope and determination, trusting in the promise of redemption and the ultimate reward at life's journey's end.

Did you know? Certain vices we indulge in can temporarily dull the guilt. However, it smites in all its horrifying power after the happy hour ends.

chapters.title: God can shield us from anxiety
chapters.text:
Even if we don’t see God, we are constantly reminded of His eternal presence when we encounter a contagiously calm person. It is a moment of elevation, and it helps us realize that God is omnipotent. He is in control.

You are not alone — always remember that God is near. At times, you may feel like He abandoned you. Nevertheless, there is never a time when you are on your own. God promised to be there for His people and will always keep it. For instance, God offered reassurance to Abraham, emphasizing His role as a protector. He remained with David and Jacob despite their moral failings. Elijah, who sometimes doubted, also found God constantly by his side.

One of the names given to God, “Immanuel,” encapsulates this idea of divine companionship. It literally translates to “God with us,” signifying a constant divine presence amidst human trials and tribulations, offering guidance and support regardless of one's actions. God became flesh, then sin, and afterward, He defeated the grave. God is with us today in the form of spirit. He guides and teaches us, comforting our hearts in the darkest times. Remember that isolation will leave you alone, facing the mightiness of chaos. It will make your anxiety levels rise. So, be the person who finds comfort in the presence of God.

God loves the sound of your voice. Always. He doesn’t hide when you call. He hears your prayers. ~ Max Lucado

Don't be afraid to pray about different things, even the smallest ones, but be humble and specific in your request. This way, you will more easily notice the response to your prayer. Moreover, it will help to alleviate your anxiety as it often has some undefined source. When you face your fears, you are halfway to conquering them.

Prayer helps us establish inner peace and project it onto the world around us.

Anytime you experience anxiety, recall the Bible entry about Palm Sunday. Citizens of Jerusalem cast their garments and placed them on the path of Jesus. Similarly, we should remove our worries and replace them with hope of the Lord's help. Don’t hesitate to turn to Him.

chapters.title: An antidote to unexpected causes of anxiety
chapters.text:
Geography tells us that the largest rivers in the world are the Nile, Amazon, and Mississippi. The wider the river, the harder it is to cross it. In human terms, the widest river is the “If Only” one. Today, many people dwell on the riverbank of “If Only,” convinced it is the very thing that separates them from a good life.

People wish for the things they don’t have and are unlikely to get. We want to be slimmer, have more money, and see other people but are afraid to break up with the ones we are dating now. People with straight hair wish they had curly locks, and vice versa. We falsely believe that getting these things will finally make us happy.

When you clear away all your unembodied wishes, you reach the root of your anxiety.

Do you see yourself standing on the shore of the If Only river? If you are, this is one of the reasons for your anxiety. You do your best and rush to cross the river to find the seemingly promised happiness. Mostly, we go for the wrong things and set unrealistic goals. Therefore, the crossing gets more challenging, and we become increasingly apprehensive.

It's high time to recall an eternal truth: instead of complaining about what we lack, we should be grateful for what we have. Apostle Paul advocated for a life where anxiety is countered by prayer, supplication, and gratitude. This approach suggests that communicating one's needs to a higher power, coupled with thankfulness, brings peace beyond human understanding, safeguarding both heart and mind.

Thankfulness is a cornerstone of life satisfaction. It is how aware you are of the benefits of life. Research has shown that practicing gratitude produces positive emotions and changes our behavior, making us more compassionate and tolerant. Moreover, appreciation increases our selflessness and improves self-confidence, quality of sleep, and relationships. Thankfulness also lays the foundation of our positive mindset if we express it; that's why a gratitude journal is so beneficial.

Did you know? When you practice genuine gratitude, you stop entertaining unrealistic expectations of yourself.

chapters.title: The cornerstone of healthy thinking
chapters.text:
Overcoming anxiety involves adopting a mindset of healthy thinking, as the real challenge lies in the perspective you choose to take. Often, the issue isn't the problem itself but how it's perceived. The devil is aware of this paradox. That is why he incessantly tries to make us magnify whatever little discomfort we might be experiencing.

These hostile forces, often personified as Satan, are known for bringing despair. Historical and religious texts provide examples like Judas, whose life ended tragically. Satan spreads corruption of body and mind wherever he aims. His evil deeds are like termites gnawing at wood; they will try to eat you from the inside out. By instilling a belief that the world is a bleak and joyless place, these evil forces aim to consume individuals with irrational and overstated thoughts, leading them to a symbolic place of darkness and isolation.

The devil may try to knock you off balance, but only you control your thoughts.

In the biblical narrative recounted in Matthew 14:28-30, Peter's experience on the water serves as a profound lesson in faith and focus. It describes how the Apostle, upon seeing Jesus walking on water, asked for the command to join him. Jesus let him do it, and Peter walked on water as long as his attention remained on Jesus. However, the moment his focus shifted to the surrounding storm, fear overwhelmed him, leading to his sinking. He had to call out to Jesus for rescue.

This incident highlights a significant spiritual truth: maintaining focus on faith, represented by Jesus, enables you to achieve what seems impossible. Conversely, when your attention is diverted toward life's challenges and fears, symbolized by the storm, it can result in a metaphorical sinking.

The teachings of Jesus emphasize the certainty and power of faith in addressing life's challenges, particularly anxiety and fear. Jesus' words in Matthew 7:7 — “Ask, and it will be given to you” — are presented as an unequivocal promise devoid of any doubt or uncertainty. This passage is often interpreted as an encouragement for believers to turn to prayer and faith, especially in times of distress, such as during panic or anxiety attacks.

So, find a prayer that reflects your situation and use it in times of uncertainty. Let a higher power take care of your worrisome thoughts and replace them with grateful ones.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Anxiety is an ambient noise that surrounds your thoughts and prevents your mind from hearing them. Just like any other sound, you can make it dissolve by turning down the source that produces it. Our anxieties grow out of our unrealistic expectations of ourselves and imposed dreams. Satan’s deeds magnify whatever doubts and fears we might experience.

We can try to battle anxiety alone, but we often fail, and it leads us to feel guilt. However, our endeavors will be much more successful when we let God help us. He is ever-present, and His grace is abundant. We need to be open and ready to share it. Instead of drowning in the guilt of being incomplete, we should be grateful for everything we have.

No matter how hard we try, each of us can fall short. It is human to err, but God doesn’t stop loving or caring for us. Moreover, He is the one who died for us so that we could be freed from our sins. And whenever we are ready to ask for His help and forgiveness, He will be there for us. So, we should never forget to thank Him.

Try this

Anxiety is the fear of events that haven’t even taken place yet. The next time you find yourself in an endless cycle of worry, pause the restlessness of your mind for a moment. Concentrate on the things that are true about you. It will help ground your thoughts and attach them to something real. For example:

“I am Mark, 25 years old; I am lying awake in my comfortable double bed covered with nice gray IKEA sheets. I am not sure what to do tomorrow because I am between jobs. This situation is what makes me anxious every night.”

Keep analyzing your state of mind until you locate what bothers you.

Read it till the end?
